stories.title: Open Championship Round 1 Underway — Scheffler Early Leader

x_posts.label: Post 1-1
x_posts.text:
The 154th Open Championship is LIVE at Royal Birkdale. 🏆

The Claret Jug is on the line. Scheffler is playing. McIlroy is playing. Fleetwood is playing at home.

Best major of the year. Every. Single. Time.

Who wins The Open? 🏴󠁧󠁢󠁥󠁮󠁧󠁿

x_posts.label: Post 1-2
x_posts.text:
Early Open Championship leaderboard at Royal Birkdale:

Scottie Scheffler showing -4 early (including a 43-foot putt at No. 6).

Thomas Detry, Alex Smalley, Dan Brown among early clubhouse leaders.

McIlroy and Fleetwood still to come in the afternoon. The Claret Jug hunt is on.

facebook.long_form:
The 154th Open Championship is here. The Claret Jug is on the line at Royal Birkdale in Southport, England — and it's already producing must-watch golf.

Scottie Scheffler, the defending champion and world No. 1, is off to an impressive start. He moved to -4 early in his first round, including sinking a remarkable 43-foot putt at the sixth hole to separate himself from the morning chasing pack. Thomas Detry of Belgium, Alex Smalley, and Dan Brown were among the early clubhouse leaders, all at -3.

The afternoon wave brings the marquee names: Rory McIlroy tees off at 15:15 BST alongside Xander Schauffele and Matthew Fitzpatrick. Tommy Fleetwood plays the morning side with Jordan Spieth and Jon Rahm — an extraordinary grouping that has the home crowd buzzing.

The 154th Open runs through Sunday July 19. But Round 1 on Thursday is where the tone is set, the early momentum is established, and the contenders separate themselves from the field. Conditions at Royal Birkdale are classic links — firm fairways, coastal wind, thick rough bordering narrow corridors.

This is what golf is for.
facebook.image_caption: The Open is underway. Scheffler early leader at Royal Birkdale. McIlroy and Fleetwood still on deck. The Claret Jug hunt begins. 🏆🏴󠁧󠁢󠁥󠁮󠁧󠁿

article.headline: Open Championship Round 1: Scheffler Charges to Early Lead at Royal Birkdale While McIlroy, Fleetwood Wait in the Wings

stories.title: Tommy Fleetwood's Hometown Major — Father Caddying, No English Winner Since Faldo 1992

x_posts.label: Post 2-1
x_posts.text:
Tommy Fleetwood is playing THE most important round of his career.

He's from Southport. He grew up near Royal Birkdale. His father is on his bag this week.

No English player has won The Open since Nick Faldo in 1992.

Fleetwood has a chance to end that 34-year drought. TODAY.

x_posts.label: Post 2-2
x_posts.text:
Tommy Fleetwood. Southport native. Father caddying. 34-year English drought.

He skipped the Scottish Open to prepare for Royal Birkdale — his home course.

If there's one round where the crowd, the emotion, and the golf can all come together...

It's today. In his backyard.

facebook.long_form:
Few moments in golf carry the emotional weight of what Tommy Fleetwood is experiencing at Royal Birkdale this week.

Fleetwood was born and raised in Southport, England. Royal Birkdale Golf Club sits in his hometown. He grew up near these fairways and rough patches, near the same sea winds that batter the links landscape he has known since childhood. And this week, his father has stepped onto the bag as his caddie.

No English player has won The Open Championship since Sir Nick Faldo's third Claret Jug in 1992 — a drought of 34 years that has produced near-misses, emotional finishes, and repeated disappointments. Fleetwood has come agonizingly close to major championship glory before, finishing runner-up in unforgettable fashion. But never won one.

He entered this week at approximately +1500 odds. He skipped the Scottish Open to spend extra time preparing for Royal Birkdale specifically. He knows every blade of grass. His father is on the bag. The home crowd will roar for every birdie.

If there is ever a time, a place, and an alignment of circumstances for Tommy Fleetwood to finally claim his first major championship, it is this week. Right here. At home.

Can he do it?
facebook.image_caption: Tommy Fleetwood. Southport. Royal Birkdale. Father on the bag. 34-year English major drought. The stars are aligned for the hometown hero. 🏴󠁧󠁢󠁥󠁮󠁧󠁿⛳

article.headline: Tommy Fleetwood at Royal Birkdale: A Hometown Major, a Father on the Bag, and a 34-Year English Drought

stories.title: McIlroy Chasing 7th Major — Would Set European Record at Birkdale

x_posts.label: Post 3-1
x_posts.text:
Rory McIlroy is ON THE COURSE at Royal Birkdale RIGHT NOW.

Six major titles. Back-to-back Masters wins in 2025 and 2026.

A 7th major would give him the European record — breaking his tie with Nick Faldo.

History is on the line at The Open Championship. 🏆

x_posts.label: Post 3-2
x_posts.text:
McIlroy's Open quest: he last won the Claret Jug at Hoylake in 2014.

12 years later, at Royal Birkdale, he has a chance at 7 majors — the most ever by a European.

The course is set. The crowd is ready. Is Rory?

facebook.long_form:
Rory McIlroy is on the golf course at Royal Birkdale right now — and the stakes for his round could not be higher.

McIlroy enters the 2026 Open Championship with six major championships: his historic four-major run early in his career plus back-to-back Masters titles in 2025 and 2026. He is the co-favorite heading into this week at approximately +700-850 odds, alongside defending champion Scottie Scheffler.

A win at The Open would give McIlroy his seventh major championship — and with it, the outright record for the most majors won by a European golfer. He currently shares the record with Sir Nick Faldo. Seven would put him alone at the top of that historic list.

His Open Championship record at Royal Birkdale has been excellent historically, and the course sets up beautifully for his driving accuracy and iron play. He tees off in the afternoon wave at 15:15 BST alongside Xander Schauffele and Matthew Fitzpatrick — a star-studded group that will command enormous galleries.

McIlroy's last Open Championship win came at Royal Liverpool (Hoylake) in 2014 — twelve years ago. He has had multiple close calls since, including a heartbreaking near-miss in 2022. The hunger is real. The talent is real. The stage is set.

History awaits.
facebook.image_caption: Rory McIlroy. 6 majors. Chasing 7 at The Open. A win at Birkdale would set the European record — most majors by any European golfer ever. Is today the day? 🏆

article.headline: Rory McIlroy at The Open: Seven Majors, a European Record, and the Claret Jug He Hasn't Lifted Since 2014

stories.title: LIV Golf Running on Loans to Complete 2026 Season

x_posts.label: Post 4
x_posts.text:
LIV Golf is running on LOANS to finish the 2026 season.

Saudi Arabia's PIF filed a debenture June 4. Only $200M of $600M committed has been provided.

LIV spends ~$100M/month. Four events remain.

The CEO won't guarantee all four happen. This is a crisis.

facebook.long_form:
The financial story surrounding LIV Golf has taken a serious turn — and it's one that every golf fan should understand.

LIV Golf is no longer funded by direct capital investment from Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund. Instead, the PIF filed a debenture on June 4, 2026 — a legal document that secures a loan against LIV Golf's assets. The league is, in short, borrowing money to operate, with PIF acting as the lender rather than the investor.

Reports indicate that PIF has provided approximately $200 million of the roughly $600 million it had committed to fund the remainder of the 2026 campaign. LIV Golf spends an estimated $100 million per month. That math means the money runs out quickly.

Four events remain on the 2026 LIV schedule: LIV Golf UK at JCB Golf & Country Club (July 23-26), LIV Golf New York (August 6-9), LIV Golf Indianapolis (August 20-23), and the $40 million LIV Team Championship in Michigan (August 27-30). LIV's CEO has stopped short of guaranteeing all four events will take place.

The league has been seeking PE investors and alternative funding sources. Without a resolution, the question hanging over every LIV event is whether the next one happens at all.

What do you think — can LIV Golf survive beyond 2026?
facebook.image_caption: LIV Golf is running on loans. PIF provided $200M of $600M committed. Four events remain. CEO won't guarantee all four happen. The league faces a financial reckoning.

article.headline: LIV Golf Is Running on Loans to Finish the 2026 Season — and Four Events Hang in the Balance

stories.title: Haeran Ryu's Historic LPGA Run — Back-to-Back Majors, Scottish Open Coming

x_posts.label: Post 5
x_posts.text:
Haeran Ryu has won back-to-back LPGA majors.

KPMG Women's PGA. Then Evian — beating Brooke Henderson in a playoff.

Her Round 3 at Evian: a 60. The lowest round in LPGA major history.

The most dominant women's golfer on the planet right now. 🏆

facebook.long_form:
While the golf world's attention is focused on Royal Birkdale this week, it's worth pausing to acknowledge what Haeran Ryu has just accomplished on the women's tour.

Ryu has now won back-to-back LPGA major championships. She claimed the KPMG Women's PGA Championship earlier this summer, then won the Amundi Evian Championship on July 12 in a playoff over Brooke Henderson. Two consecutive majors. The most dominant stretch on the LPGA Tour in recent memory.

What makes Ryu's Evian win even more remarkable is the third round she played to set it up. She carded a 60 — a 11-under score that stands as the lowest single round in the history of LPGA major championship golf. The previous records had stood for years. She obliterated them.

Top-ranked Nelly Korda missed the cut at Evian, underscoring how Ryu has transcended the field at the game's biggest moments.

The LPGA's next major event is the ISPS HANDA Women's Scottish Open at Dundonald Links, July 23-26, before the schedule turns to the US Women's Open. Ryu heads into that stretch as the undisputed No. 1 performer on tour.

How far can her run go?
facebook.image_caption: Haeran Ryu: KPMG champ. Evian champ. Back-to-back LPGA majors. Her Round 3 score of 60 is the lowest in LPGA major history. What a player. 🏆

article.headline: Haeran Ryu Has Won Back-to-Back LPGA Majors — and Her Evian Round of 60 Is Historic
